title: 12 Fundamental Existential Prerequisites (12FEP) — Längle-Grounded Codebook
purpose: Score participant testimony from psychological interventions on 12 existential prerequisites derived from Längle’s four Existential Fundamental Motivations (EFMs). All constructs, quotes, and language are drawn from Längle’s works.

anchors.0: Not present; opposite experience predominates (e.g., “restlessness, insecurity and fear”; “longing… coldness… depression”; “solitude… shame”; “emptiness… despair”).
anchors.1: Barely present; fleeting or fragile signs; coping reactions dominate (e.g., “avoidance or flight… overactivity… destructive aggression… denial”; “regression”).
anchors.2: Partly present; intermittent signs without stable appropriation (“it is not enough to find… I also have to… accept”).
anchors.3: Moderately present; clear signs plus some active stance (e.g., “to accept… and to endure”; “turn to… get into touch”).
anchors.4: Strongly present; consistent experience and appropriation with minor gaps.
anchors.5: Fully present; stable experience and corresponding active stance (e.g., “to be there and not to flee”; “consent to being touched”; “say ‘yes to myself’”; “attitude of openness… ‘What does this hour want from me?’”).

global_instructions_for_llms: Use only concepts and phrasing grounded in Längle’s texts; when justifying any score, include a short participant excerpt and a direct Längle quote with citation.
Prioritize the dialogical and phenomenological framing of motivation across the four cornerstones (world, life, person, meaning).

Treat meaning as “the most valuable, realistic possibility of the given situation, for which I feel I should decide myself,” and apply this definition when identifying forward-looking value commitments.

When evidence is mixed, score the lower anchor unless the participant shows the relevant active stance (accept/endure; turn to/consent to being touched; say yes to self/encounter/delimit; openness to what life asks).
Use negative indicators and coping patterns to justify low scores (0–1).

example.participant_excerpt: I protect Friday evenings for my kids—nothing overrides that.
presence_or_absence_indicators_matched: Deliberately taking time for what is valuable
example.langle_anchor_quote_with_citation: “What do I take time for?… To take time for something means to give away a part of one’s life.”
example.rationale_linking_excerpt_to_quote: The participant’s non-negotiable practice exemplifies giving away life-time to valuable relationships.

items.name: Protection
anchor_quotes: “This demands three things: protection, space and support.”
“Do I enjoy protection, acceptance, do I feel at home somewhere?”
“The sum of these experiences of trust is the fundamental trust.”
indicators_present: Describes “trust in the world and confidence in myself.”
indicators_absent: Relies on coping like avoidance/flight when conditions unmet.
items.active_stance_quote: “To accept means to… trust the protection; in short ‘to be there’ and not to flee.”

scoring_guidance.0_1: No felt protection; fear dominates; flight/avoidance patterns.
scoring_guidance.2_3: Some safety experiences without stable acceptance or reliance.
scoring_guidance.4_5: Stable felt safety + explicit acceptance (“to be there and not to flee”).

items.name: Space
anchor_quotes: “Do I have enough space for being there?”
“To accept means to be ready to occupy the space.”
indicators_present: States room to exist/act; readiness to “occupy the space.”
items.active_stance_quote: “To accept… to be ready to occupy the space.”

scoring_guidance.4_5: Consistently takes and holds space in life contexts.

items.name: Support
anchor_quotes: “Where do I find support in my life?”
“To rely on the support… ‘to be there’ and not to flee.”
indicators_present: Identifies and relies on supports (people, structures, faith).
indicators_absent: Groundlessness; escalating fear/anxiety when coping fails.
items.active_stance_quote: “To rely on the support… and… not to flee.”

scoring_guidance.4_5: Stable supports and explicit reliance in action/decisions.

items.name: Relationship
items.fundamental_question: “I am alive—do I like this fact?”
anchor_quotes: “In order to seize my life, to love it, I need three things: relationship, time and closeness.”
“Do I have relationships, in which I feel closeness… and in which I experience community?”
indicators_present: Turns toward others and engages (“turn to… get into touch”).
items.active_stance_quote: “I seize life… when I turn to other people… get into touch…”

scoring_guidance.4_5: Sustained, reciprocal relationships with active turning-toward.

items.name: Time
items.fundamental_question: “I am alive—do I like this fact?”
anchor_quotes: “What do I take time for?… To take time for something means to give away a part of one’s life.”
indicators_present: Deliberately gives time to valuable things; protects time for what is “worthy.”
indicators_absent: Life feels stale via “shallowness of daily life” / lifestyle negligence.
items.active_stance_quote: “To take time… means to give away a part of one’s life.”

scoring_guidance.0_1: No protected time for what is valuable; staleness noted.

items.name: Closeness
items.fundamental_question: “I am alive—do I like this fact?”
anchor_quotes: “Can I feel close… Can I admit the closeness of someone else?”
“If life is to make me move freely, my consent to being touched… is necessary.”
indicators_present: Readiness to be moved (“This ‘to turn to’ will make life vibrate within me”).
indicators_absent: Basic coping is “regression”; if unresolved, depression.
items.active_stance_quote: “My consent to being touched (to feeling) is necessary.”

scoring_guidance.4_5: Open, sustained closeness and affective responsiveness.

items.name: Attention
items.fundamental_question: “I am myself—may I be like this?”
anchor_quotes: “By whom am I seen? Who considers my uniqueness and respects my boundaries?”
“Encounter represents the necessary bridge…”
indicators_present: Feels seen and boundary-respected; participates in genuine encounter.
indicators_absent: “Solitude… need to hide behind the shame.”

scoring_guidance.4_5: Consistent feeling of being seen with self-affirming stance.

items.name: Justice
items.fundamental_question: “I am myself—may I be like this?”
anchor_quotes: “Do people do me justice?”
Active stance: “stand by my own… refuse whatever does not correspond to myself.”
indicators_present: Speaks to fairness/right to be; sets boundaries congruently.
items.active_stance_quote: “Stand by my own… refuse whatever does not correspond to myself.”

scoring_guidance.2_3: Some fairness experiences; boundaries inconsistently held.
scoring_guidance.4_5: Stable sense of justice with consistent, ethical self-positioning.

items.name: Appreciation
items.fundamental_question: “I am myself—may I be like this?”
anchor_quotes: “For what am I appreciated—for what can I appreciate myself?… The sum of these experiences builds… the self-esteem.”
indicators_absent: When unmet: solitude, shame; personality issues.

scoring_guidance.4_5: Stable appreciation of self and from others; self-respect evident.

items.name: Field of Activity
items.fundamental_question: “I am there—for what is it good?”
anchor_quotes: “Is there a place where I feel needed, where I can be productive?”
indicators_present: Names a concrete arena of contribution → capable of “dedication and action.”
indicators_absent: “Feeling of emptiness, frustration, even despair… frequently addiction.”
items.active_stance_quote: “My active part… is to… examine whether what I am doing is really a good thing.”

scoring_guidance.0_1: No felt place of being needed; emptiness/despair.
scoring_guidance.4_5: Stable field with sustained contribution and dedication.

items.name: Structural Context
items.fundamental_question: “I am there—for what is it good?”
anchor_quotes: “Do I see and experience myself in a larger context that provides structure and orientation… Where I want to be integrated?”
Illustrative stance: “I am working at a cathedral.” (orientation/integration).
indicators_present: Frames activity within wider purposes/communities; orientation and integration expressed.
items.active_stance_quote: “Attitude of openness… ‘What does this hour want from me…?’”

scoring_guidance.2_3: Some context awareness without consistent integration.
scoring_guidance.4_5: Clear, lived orientation within a larger whole.

items.name: Value to be Realized
items.fundamental_question: “I am there—for what is it good?”
anchor_quotes: “Is there anything that should still be realized in my life?”
Meaning is “the most valuable, realistic possibility of the given situation, for which I feel I should decide myself.”
indicators_present: Names a specific to-be-realized value/possibility; lives an “attitude of openness” to what is asked now.
items.active_stance_quote: “Examine whether what I am doing is really a good thing… If I act accordingly, my existence will be fulfilling.”

scoring_guidance.0_1: No valued possibility in view; emptiness/despair.
scoring_guidance.2_3: Vague or unstable aims without tested goodness.
scoring_guidance.4_5: Specific, realistic value embraced with reflective openness.

negative_indicators_and_coping.E1_World: If unmet → “restlessness, insecurity and fear”; coping: “avoidance or flight… overactivity… destructive aggression… denial.”
negative_indicators_and_coping.E2_Life: If unmet → “longing… coldness… depression”; basic coping: “regression.”
negative_indicators_and_coping.E4_Meaning: If unmet → “emptiness, frustration, even despair… frequently addiction.”

apa_citations: Längle, A. (2002). Existential fundamental motivation. In Motivation, consciousness and self-regulation (pp. 27–42). Nova Science Publishers.

Längle, A. (2011). The existential fundamental motivations structuring the motivational process. In D. A. Leontiev (Ed.), Motivation, consciousness and self-regulation (pp. 27–42). Nova Science Publishers.
